The statute defines eligible convictions as current/former Class E crimes except Chapter 11 offenses, plus specified marijuana-related convictions committed before January 30, 2017. (

For the general adult sealing route, Wilma must confirm all of these:
Eligible conviction Must be an eligible conviction under 2261(6)
Waiting period At least 4 years since the person fully satisfied the sentence
Maine convictions after completion No later Maine criminal conviction
Deferred disposition after completion No later Maine criminal charge dismissed as a result of deferred disposition
Out-of-state / other jurisdiction conviction No later conviction in another jurisdiction
Pending charges No pending criminal charges in Maine or another jurisdiction
These prerequisites are in 15 M.R.S. 2262. (

"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2262.html") CR-218 also asks the filer to certify that at least 4 years have passed since completing all sentencing components, including incarceration, probation, administrative release, license suspension, fines, restitution, and community service.

Maines CR-218 adult sealing process is for specific convictions. Non-conviction records are governed differently through Maines criminal-history confidentiality framework, not the same CR-218 conviction-sealing motion. Maines definition of confidential criminal history record information includes several non-conviction outcomes: arrest/summons information older than one year with no active prosecution, law enforcement non-referral, prosecutor declined prosecution, grand jury no bill, dismissal with prejudice or final prosecutor dismissal not as part of a plea agreement, acquittal, mistrial with prejudice, jurisdictional termination, and full/free pardon. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/16/title16sec703.html")
Waiting period At least 3 years after discharge from the juvenile disposition
Juvenile Class D/E/civil-type juvenile crime Juvenile automatic sealing may apply Sealed after discharge notice, generally within five business days
General adult Class E / qualifying marijuana conviction sealing 4 years After all sentence alternatives are fully satisfied
Former Class E engaging in prostitution 1 year After all sentence alternatives are fully satisfied
Sex trafficking / sexual exploitation survivor route No ordinary waiting period Motion may be filed any time after conviction
Juvenile murder/Class A/B/C/OUI petition 3 years After discharge from juvenile disposition

Non-conviction CHRI confidentiality Varies by disposition Many listed non-conviction outcomes become confidential by statute, but arrest/summons without disposition generally requires more than 1 year and no active prosecution

Sealing does not erase the juvenile record. Maine courts, criminal justice agencies, the juvenile, and the juveniles designee can still access sealed juvenile records; the public generally cannot. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html") A person with sealed juvenile records may answer most non-court/non-criminal-justice inquiries as if the juvenile crime never occurred, but firearm prohibitions are not removed by sealing. pathways.label: Sex-trafficking / sexual-exploitation-survivor sealing
pathways.summary: Maine enacted Public Law 2025, Chapter 513, effective without the Governors signature on January 11, 2026, creating a broader survivor-based sealing route for victims of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/legis/bills/display_ps.asp?LD=1871&snum=132") Under the new 2262-B route, criminal history record information relating to any current or former crime may be sealed if the person establishes: Victim status The person was a victim of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation Causal connection The crime of conviction was a substantial result of the trafficking or exploitation Timing Motion may be filed at any…
ruleClauses: Maine enacted Public Law 2025, Chapter 513, effective without the Governors signature on January 11, 2026, creating a broader survivor-based sealing route for victims of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/legis/bills/display_ps.asp?LD=1871&snum=132")
Under the new 2262-B route, criminal history record information relating to any current or former crime may be sealed if the person establishes:
Victim status The person was a victim of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation
Causal connection The crime of conviction was a substantial result of the trafficking or exploitation
Timing Motion may be filed at any time after conviction
The enacted text says the route applies to a conviction for any current or former crime, and the motion may be filed at any time after the conviction is entered.
the filer and witnesses may participate remotely unless the court finds in-person participation is required;
official documentation is not required;
if official documentation is presented, it creates a presumption that the persons participation in the offense was substantially the result of trafficking/exploitation;

supporting documentation may include federal/state/tribal/local records, immigration-related certification/approval notices, affidavits or sworn testimony from clergy, medical professionals, victim-services staff, or other professionals, and any other credible/probative evidence the court accepts.

The court generally holds a hearing on sealing motions, but for a 2262-B motion, the court may grant the motion without a hearing if the State consents. The person must establish the prerequisites by a preponderance of the evidence.
This route involves trauma, trafficking, and exploitation. Wilma should not force graphic details. Use gentle, minimal questions:
Was this conviction connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival sex, or someone forcing or pressuring you into conduct connected to the charge?
Then route to manual/legal review.

ruleClauses: Maine juvenile records are separate from adult CR-218 sealing. Maines juvenile records page says some juvenile records are automatically sealed after completion of the court disposition, while others require a petition. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html")

For a juvenile adjudicated of a Class D or Class E juvenile crime, or a juvenile crime that would be a civil offense if committed by an adult, the court automatically seals the juvenile court record within 5 business days after a Notice of Discharge is filed with the Juvenile Court. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html") The statute similarly provides automatic sealing after discharge notice for juvenile crimes other than murder/Class A/B/C/OUI categories. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")
For juvenile adjudications that would be murder, Class A, Class B, Class C, or OUI if committed by an adult, the person may petition to seal if:
Waiting period At least 3 years after discharge from the juvenile disposition
New juvenile/adult record No later juvenile adjudication or adult conviction since disposition
Pending matters No current juvenile or adult proceedings pending
The court may grant unless the publics right to information substantially outweighs the juveniles privacy interest. (
"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")

Sealing does not erase the juvenile record. Maine courts, criminal justice agencies, the juvenile, and the juveniles designee can still access sealed juvenile records; the public generally cannot. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html") A person with sealed juvenile records may answer most non-court/non-criminal-justice inquiries as if the juvenile crime never occurred, but firearm prohibitions are not removed by sealing. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")

Conviction tied to sex trafficking or sexual exploitation Survivor sealing route under 15 M.R.S. 2262-B / Public Law 513 Any current or former crime may qualify if the conviction was a substantial result of trafficking/exploitation
Adult felony conviction not tied to trafficking/exploitation Usually not eligible under the basic CR-218 route Maines basic adult sealing route is narrow
Adult Class D conviction not marijuana-related Usually not eligible under basic CR-218 Check survivor route only if trafficking/exploitation applies
Arrest / charge with no conviction Do not use CR-218 as default Check Maine confidential criminal-history rules, disposition, and court-record status
Pardon Not expungement Full/free pardon makes the conviction confidential criminal-history information; not wiped clean
Juvenile Class D/E/civil-type juvenile crime Juvenile automatic sealing may apply Sealed after discharge notice, generally within five business days
Juvenile murder/Class A/B/C/OUI adjudication Juvenile petition to seal 3-year wait + no new adjudication/conviction + no pending proceedings

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Sealing
In Maine, sealing makes the conviction confidential criminal history record information and restricts public dissemination. A sealed conviction generally should not show to ordinary third parties like employers, landlords, lenders, and schools, but it is still available to criminal justice agencies, courts, licensing agencies, financial regulators, some fingerprint-based background-check entities, victims, and other statutorily authorized recipients. (

"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2265.html")
Expungement
For Wilmas Maine workflow, avoid saying
expungement.
Maine court materials state directly that the record is not completely erased and that Maine does not have
expungement
in the ordinary sense. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/help/criminal/sealing-prostitution.html")
Wilma rule: Say
seal from most public access,
not
delete,

erase,
or
wipe clean.

sourceSections.title: Official forms Wilma should know
sourceSections.text:
Route Official form
General adult conviction sealing CR-218 - Motion to Seal Criminal History
Former Class E engaging in prostitution CR-289 - Motion to Seal Conviction for Engaging in Prostitution
Juvenile sealing petition JV-043 - Petition to Seal Juvenile Case Records
Fee waiver CV-067 - Application to Proceed Without Payment of Fees
Fee waiver financial affidavit CV-191 - Financial Affidavit
The Maine Judicial Branch forms list includes CR-218 Motion to Seal Criminal History, revised 7/1/2024, and CR-289 Motion to Seal Conviction for Engaging in Prostitution, revised 10/1/2024. (

"https://mjbportal.courts.maine.gov/CourtForms/FormsLists/Index") CR-218 itself states that the motion is brought under 15 M.R.S. 2263-2264 and requires the defendant to state the conviction, eligibility section, 4-year completion period, no later Maine conviction/deferred-disposition dismissal, no later out-of-state conviction, and no pending charges.

sourceSections.title: Adult conviction sealing - general route
sourceSections.text:
The general adult sealing route is for a specific criminal conviction, not a whole-record sweep. A person may file a written motion in the underlying criminal proceeding, and the motion must address each statutory prerequisite. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2263.html")
Eligible convictions
Maines general definition of
eligible criminal conviction
includes:

Category Rule
Current or former Class E crime Eligible, except current/former Class E crimes under Title 17-A, Chapter 11
Certain marijuana convictions committed before Jan. 30, 2017 Eligible if they fall within the listed marijuana cultivation/possession categories in 2261(6)(B)
The statute defines eligible convictions as current/former Class E crimes except Chapter 11 offenses, plus specified marijuana-related convictions committed before January 30, 2017. (

"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2261.html")
Required prerequisites
For the general adult sealing route, Wilma must confirm all of these:

Requirement Maine rule
Eligible conviction Must be an eligible conviction under 2261(6)
Waiting period At least 4 years since the person fully satisfied the sentence
Maine convictions after completion No later Maine criminal conviction
Deferred disposition after completion No later Maine criminal charge dismissed as a result of deferred disposition
Out-of-state / other jurisdiction conviction No later conviction in another jurisdiction
Pending charges No pending criminal charges in Maine or another jurisdiction
These prerequisites are in 15 M.R.S. 2262. (

"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2262.html") CR-218 also asks the filer to certify that at least 4 years have passed since completing all sentencing components, including incarceration, probation, administrative release, license suspension, fines, restitution, and community service.
Wilma general adult sealing logic
Wilma should ask:
1 Was this a conviction, not just an arrest?
2 What was the exact offense and class?
3 Was it a current/former Class E offense?
4 Was it under Title 17-A, Chapter 11? If yes, flag likely ineligible under the general route.
5 Was it one of the listed pre-2017 marijuana convictions?
6 When were all sentence terms fully completed?
7 Any later Maine convictions?
8 Any later deferred-disposition dismissal?
9 Any later out-of-state/federal/tribal convictions?
10 Any pending criminal charges?
Wilma should not say eligible unless all prerequisites are confirmed.

sourceSections.title: Sex trafficking / sexual exploitation survivor sealing - 2026 update
sourceSections.text:
Maine enacted Public Law 2025, Chapter 513, effective without the Governors signature on January 11, 2026, creating a broader survivor-based sealing route for victims of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/legis/bills/display_ps.asp?LD=1871&snum=132")
Eligibility
Under the new 2262-B route, criminal history record information relating to any current or former crime may be sealed if the person establishes:

Requirement Rule
Victim status The person was a victim of sex trafficking or sexual exploitation
Causal connection The crime of conviction was a substantial result of the trafficking or exploitation
Timing Motion may be filed at any time after conviction
The enacted text says the route applies to a conviction for any current or former crime, and the motion may be filed at any time after the conviction is entered.
Evidence rules
For 2262-B motions:

the filer and witnesses may participate remotely unless the court finds in-person participation is required;
official documentation is not required;
if official documentation is presented, it creates a presumption that the persons participation in the offense was substantially the result of trafficking/exploitation;
supporting documentation may include federal/state/tribal/local records, immigration-related certification/approval notices, affidavits or sworn testimony from clergy, medical professionals, victim-services staff, or other professionals, and any other credible/probative evidence the court accepts.
Hearing rule
The court generally holds a hearing on sealing motions, but for a 2262-B motion, the court may grant the motion without a hearing if the State consents. The person must establish the prerequisites by a preponderance of the evidence.
Wilma safety rule
This route involves trauma, trafficking, and exploitation. Wilma should not force graphic details. Use gentle, minimal questions:

Was this conviction connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival sex, or someone forcing or pressuring you into conduct connected to the charge?

Then route to manual/legal review.

sourceSections.title: Adult non-conviction records
sourceSections.text:
Maines CR-218 adult sealing process is for specific convictions. Non-conviction records are governed differently through Maines criminal-history confidentiality framework, not the same CR-218 conviction-sealing motion. Maines definition of confidential criminal history record information includes several non-conviction outcomes: arrest/summons information older than one year with no active prosecution, law enforcement non-referral, prosecutor declined prosecution, grand jury no bill, dismissal with prejudice or final prosecutor dismissal not as part of a plea agreement, acquittal, mistrial with prejudice, jurisdictional termination, and full/free pardon. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/16/title16sec703.html")
Confidential criminal history record information may be disseminated only to listed recipients, but Maine law still allows certain specific-date inquiries about whether a named person was arrested, detained, summonsed, or had formal charges initiated on a specific date. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/16/title16sec705.html")
Wilma routing rule: For no-conviction Maine cases, do not generate CR-218. Instead, collect the disposition and flag as:

likely confidential criminal-history route,

verify SBI record status,
and

court-record access may need separate review.

sourceSections.title: Pardon route
sourceSections.text:
A full and free pardon in Maine does not wipe the record clean. Maines Department of Corrections Pardon Board states that Maine does not expunge or erase criminal records and that a pardon makes the pardoned conviction confidential criminal-history information, available only under limited statutory conditions. ("https://www.maine.gov/corrections/pardonboard?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma rule: If the user says
I was pardoned,
do not promise expungement. Route to pardon-confidentiality verification and SBI/FBI-record review.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile sealing
sourceSections.text:
Maine juvenile records are separate from adult CR-218 sealing. Maines juvenile records page says some juvenile records are automatically sealed after completion of the court disposition, while others require a petition. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html")
Automatic juvenile sealing
For a juvenile adjudicated of a Class D or Class E juvenile crime, or a juvenile crime that would be a civil offense if committed by an adult, the court automatically seals the juvenile court record within 5 business days after a Notice of Discharge is filed with the Juvenile Court. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html") The statute similarly provides automatic sealing after discharge notice for juvenile crimes other than murder/Class A/B/C/OUI categories. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")
Juvenile petition to seal
For juvenile adjudications that would be murder, Class A, Class B, Class C, or OUI if committed by an adult, the person may petition to seal if:

Requirement Rule
Waiting period At least 3 years after discharge from the juvenile disposition
New juvenile/adult record No later juvenile adjudication or adult conviction since disposition
Pending matters No current juvenile or adult proceedings pending
The court may grant unless the publics right to information substantially outweighs the juveniles privacy interest. (

"https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")
Juvenile sealing effect
Sealing does not erase the juvenile record. Maine courts, criminal justice agencies, the juvenile, and the juveniles designee can still access sealed juvenile records; the public generally cannot. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/courts/juvenile/records.html") A person with sealed juvenile records may answer most non-court/non-criminal-justice inquiries as if the juvenile crime never occurred, but firearm prohibitions are not removed by sealing. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec3308-C.html")

sourceSections.title: Filing instructions
sourceSections.text:
For adult conviction sealing, file the motion in the underlying criminal proceeding. The clerk sets the motion for hearing. The person has the right to counsel but is not entitled to state-appointed counsel for this sealing motion. The prosecutorial office that handled the original case may represent the State. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/15/title15sec2264.html")
The Maine Judicial Branch says to complete CR-218 and return it to the clerks office for the court where the conviction occurred. The court schedules a hearing, reviews the statutory requirements, and grants the motion if the filer proves each requirement by a preponderance of the evidence. If granted, the court sends notice to the State Bureau of Identification, which seals the conviction and mails notice to the person. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/help/criminal/sealing.html")
For multiple 2262-B survivor-sealing motions, the 2026 law says the court consolidates multiple motions to one location.

sourceSections.title: Fees
sourceSections.text:
The current Maine court fee schedule lists a $60 fee for filing certain post-judgment motions or requests under Maine Rules of Civil Procedure 55(b)(2), 59, 60(b), 62, or 66 in both Superior Court and District Court; confirm the exact CR-218/CR-289 fee with the clerk at filing because local processing may depend on how the motion is docketed. ("https://www.courts.maine.gov/adminorders/jb-05-26.pdf")
If the user cannot afford filing fees, Wilma should surface the fee-waiver route: CV-067 Application to Proceed Without Payment of Fees and CV-191 Financial Affidavit. The official CV-067 form says the application must be filed with a completed Financial Affidavit, CV-191. ("https://mjbportal.courts.maine.gov/CourtForms/FormsLists/DownloadForm?strFormNumber=CV-067&utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.title: Record request process
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should start by helping the user identify the exact Maine record. The Maine State Police / State Bureau of Identification offers an online Maine Criminal History Record & Juvenile Crime Information Request service. It provides electronic access to criminal-history and juvenile-crime information maintained by the Maine State Police, State Bureau of Identification. The public search returns adult conviction/adjudication information and pending cases less than one year old. ("https://www.maine.gov/pcr?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
The State Bureau of Identification page says the online public criminal-history search costs $31, and a notarized background check costs $41. ("https://www.maine.gov/dps/msp/about/sbi?utm_source=chatgpt.com") A person or their attorney also has a statutory right to inspect criminal-history record information maintained by a Maine criminal justice agency and may request correction of inaccurate or incomplete information. ("https://legislature.maine.gov/statutes/16/title16sec709.html")

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake questions for Maine
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should ask in this order:
1
Was this an adult case or juvenile case?

2
Was there a conviction, or was the case dismissed, declined, no-billed, acquitted, or never charged?

3
What was the exact offense name and class?

4
What court and county handled the case?

5
What is the docket number, if you have it?

6
When were you convicted?

7
When did you finish every part of the sentence: jail, probation, administrative release, license suspension, fines, restitution, and community service?

8
Have you had any Maine convictions since then?

9
Have you had any deferred-disposition dismissal since then?

10
Have you had any convictions in another state, federal court, tribal court, or another jurisdiction since then?

11
Do you have any pending criminal charges anywhere?

12
Was this a marijuana offense committed before January 30, 2017?

13
Was this the former Class E engaging-in-prostitution offense?

14
Was the conviction connected to sex trafficking, sexual exploitation, coercion, or survival circumstances?

15
Do you need help getting your Maine SBI record first?

sourceSections.title: Maine waiting-period table
Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Maine, train Wilma with this core logic:
Maine = sealing, not expungement.Default adult form = CR-218 Motion to Seal Criminal History.General adult eligibility = current/former Class E except Chapter 11, plus listed pre-2017 marijuana convictions, with a 4-year clean period after sentence completion.Special prostitution form = CR-289, 1-year wait.New 2026 survivor route = any current/former conviction may be sealed if substantially caused by sex trafficking or sexual exploitation.Non-convictions are not CR-218 cases; treat them as confidential-history / record-status verification issues.Juvenile sealing is separate, with automatic sealing for many lower-level juvenile cases and petition sealing for serious categories.
